# Case 180: Symbol Binding Lost GNU_UNIQUE

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **Verdict** | 🟡 **COMPATIBLE_WITH_RISK** |
| **Category** | Risk |
| **Platforms** | Linux |
| **Flags** | Bad practice |
| **Detected `ChangeKind`s** | `symbol_binding_lost_unique` |
| **Source files** | `examples/case180_symbol_binding_lost_unique/` |

**Category:** Deployment Risk (ELF/Loader) | **Verdict:** COMPATIBLE_WITH_RISK

## What this case is about

```cpp
/* v1 and v2 share byte-identical source. */
template <typename T>
struct Registry {
    static T& instance() {
        static T value{};   // a function-local static inside a class template
        return value;
    }
};
```

```
v1: g++ -shared -fPIC              v1.cpp -o libv1.so   (GNU_UNIQUE, the default)
v2: g++ -shared -fPIC -fno-gnu-unique v2.cpp -o libv2.so   (ordinary weak binding)
```

`Registry<Widget>::instance()`'s function-local static has *vague linkage*:
because it lives inside a template, an identical definition could be emitted
independently by any translation unit (in this library or another) that
instantiates `Registry<Widget>`. By default, GCC marks this guard variable
`STB_GNU_UNIQUE` — a GNU ELF extension where the **dynamic linker**, not the
normal weak-symbol merge rule, guarantees exactly one definition is used
process-wide, no matter how many DSOs independently emit a copy. `v2` is
built with `-fno-gnu-unique`, falling back to ordinary weak-symbol binding.

## Why this case matters: "one instance" becomes "one instance per DSO"

`STB_GNU_UNIQUE` exists specifically for singleton-shaped vague-linkage data
like a Meyers-singleton static: if `Registry<Widget>` is also independently
instantiated by a *second* DSO loaded into the same process, GNU_UNIQUE
binding is what makes both DSOs share the **same** `Widget` instance — the
loader looks it up in a process-wide unique-symbol table by name, keyed
across every loaded object, instead of resolving it only within each DSO's
normal symbol scope. Drop `GNU_UNIQUE` and each DSO can end up with its
**own**, independent copy: two "singletons" that silently disagree.

`STB_GNU_UNIQUE` carries a second, unrelated cost even for a single DSO: the
loader's unique-symbol table pins the *defining object* in memory for the
life of the process, so a `dlopen()`'d DSO that exports a `GNU_UNIQUE` symbol
cannot be fully unloaded by `dlclose()` — losing `GNU_UNIQUE` actually
*removes* that pinning. This case is about the loss of the uniqueness
guarantee; the dlclose-inhibition side effect is the inverse trade-off worth
knowing about when reading it.

## What abicheck detects

- **`symbol_binding_lost_unique`** — the guard variable for
  `Registry<Widget>::instance()::value` moved from `STB_GNU_UNIQUE` binding
  to an ordinary weak/global binding. **Evidence tier L0** — read directly
  from the ELF symbol table's `st_info` binding field; no DWARF, no headers.
  (The opposite transition, gaining `GNU_UNIQUE` — including the common case
  of a whole release newly enabling `-fgnu-unique` — is
  `symbol_binding_became_unique`.)

**Overall verdict: COMPATIBLE_WITH_RISK** — `get_widget_id()` still returns
the same value from a single DSO; the risk only manifests in a multi-DSO
process where the same template gets instantiated more than once.

## How to reproduce

```bash
g++ -shared -fPIC -O2 v1.cpp -o libv1.so
g++ -shared -fPIC -O2 -fno-gnu-unique v2.cpp -o libv2.so
readelf -s libv1.so | grep -i UNIQUE   # OBJECT UNIQUE ... _ZZN8RegistryI6WidgetE8instanceEvE5value
readelf -s libv2.so | grep -i UNIQUE   # (nothing)

python3 -m abicheck.cli dump libv1.so -o /tmp/v1.json
python3 -m abicheck.cli dump libv2.so -o /tmp/v2.json
python3 -m abicheck.cli compare /tmp/v1.json /tmp/v2.json
# → COMPATIBLE_WITH_RISK: symbol_binding_lost_unique
```

## Real Failure Demo (illustrative — not the CI oracle)

```bash
g++ app.cpp -I. -L. -lv1 -Wl,-rpath,. -o app
./app
# get_widget_id() = 42 (expected 42)
```

The single-DSO demo above always prints the same value — that is expected;
`STB_GNU_UNIQUE`'s guarantee only becomes observable in a multi-DSO process.
To see the actual divergence, build the *same* `v1.cpp` twice under two
different SONAMEs (e.g. `liba.so` and `libb.so`), `dlopen()` both into one
process, and compare the address of `Registry<Widget>::instance()` as seen
from each: under `GNU_UNIQUE` binding (v1) both DSOs resolve to the *same*
address; under `-fno-gnu-unique` (v2), glibc versions and load order
determine whether they coincide or diverge — precisely the kind of
loader-version-dependent behavior this catalog avoids gating CI on (see
`case171_static_tls_introduced` for the same caveat). The artifact fact
above — the binding itself changing — is the deterministic, CI-graded proof.

## Mitigation

- Do not build parts of a project with `-fno-gnu-unique` and other parts
  without it — a template-heavy library and its plugins should agree on
  this flag, or singleton-shaped vague-linkage statics can silently diverge
  across the boundary.
- If a true process-wide singleton is required regardless of DSO topology,
  consider an explicit, named exported symbol instead of relying on
  vague-linkage template statics.

## References

- [GNU ELF extensions: `STB_GNU_UNIQUE`](https://www.airs.com/blog/archives/457)
- Related cases: `case128_symbol_binding_strengthened`,
  `case27_symbol_binding_weakened`
